result:
Two questions before I finalize the plan:

**1. Auth flow**: Reddit "script" app OAuth2 requires storing the account password in config. Two options:
- **Script (password grant)** — Simpler: store client_id, client_secret, username, password. No browser needed. Matches the Twitch extension pattern. Caveat: 2FA accounts won't work.
- **Refresh token flow** — One-time browser OAuth2 authorization, then store refresh_token. More secure (no password stored), supports 2FA, but requires redirect URI setup.

**2. Scope**: Should username mentions (u/botname in subreddit comments) be included in the initial implementation, or just DMs?
- **DMs + Mentions** — Poll both inbox and mentions. Mentions route as "group" chat type with subreddit context.
- **DMs only** — Simpler initial scope. Only poll inbox for private messages.

questions.question: Reddit 'script' app OAuth2 requires storing the account password in config. Should we use this approach, or would you prefer a refresh_token-based flow (requires a one-time browser auth but avoids storing the password)?

options.label: Script (password grant)
options.description: Simpler setup: store client_id, client_secret, username, password. No browser needed. Matches Twitch pattern. Note: 2FA accounts won't work.

options.label: Refresh token flow
options.description: One-time browser OAuth2 authorization, then store refresh_token. More secure (no password stored), supports 2FA accounts, but requires redirect URI setup.

questions.question: Should mentions (u/botname in subreddit comments) be included in the initial implementation, or just DMs?

options.label: DMs + Mentions
options.description: Poll both /message/inbox and /message/mentions. Mentions use 'group' chat type with subreddit context.

options.label: DMs only
options.description: Simpler initial scope. Only poll /message/inbox for private messages. Mentions can be added later.
